That mutual dependence is what generates the rule. This is not splitting the difference. It is a priority (preparation) that admits its own condition: it is only defensible if the floor beneath today's workers holds. ## Step 7 — the full argument essay

(Phone timer set: 35 minutes. Wrote the whole thing, cleaned up typos after.)

---

Every scarcity argument eventually stops being about the scarce thing and becomes a question about time: when two necessities compete for one resource, which loss can be undone, and which one is forever? The competition between irrigation and thermoelectric power for a shrinking water supply is one instance of that larger question. Both uses are enormous and nearly equal in scale — roughly 118 and 133 billion gallons withdrawn per day — and both protect something a society cannot function without. Because a failed grid takes irrigation down with it, a water-short region should guarantee power plants their minimum operating water first; but because farmland abandoned in a drought may never return, every gallon above that minimum floor must go to agriculture. This is not a compromise that makes everyone happy. It is a rule for choosing which pain is temporary.

The case for putting the power floor first rests on a dependency the agriculture-first side has to concede. Modern irrigation is not rain and ditches; it is electric pumps lifting groundwater and pushing it across fields. A region that heroically routes all its water to farms and lets the grid brown out has not saved its farms — it has handed them full water rights and no way to move the water. It has also cut power to hospitals, water-treatment plants, and homes in exactly the kind of heat wave that caused the shortage. The power floor is not a vote for electricity over food. It is the recognition that in a modern economy the food system runs *through* the grid, so protecting the grid's minimum is the first step of protecting the farms.

But the floor must stay a floor, and here is the cost my own position has to pay: any rule that lets utilities claim water first will be stretched. Power companies, in a bad year, will argue that "minimum operating water" means comfortable margins

The competition between irrigation and thermoelectric power for a shrinking water supply looks like a contest between two goods, food and electricity, but it is really a question about dependency: which system, if it fails, takes the other down with it. ## STEP 4 — FRQ 1: my synthesis essay

Every debate about the renewable grid is really one instance of a larger question: what does "feasible" mean when the obstacle to a goal is no longer technical but political — when a country knows how to do something and must decide whether it will pay for it? The sources make clear that a mostly renewable American grid sits exactly at that threshold. A mostly renewable grid is feasible and worth building, but only if the nation treats the enormous cost of storage and transmission as the price of the project rather than an argument against it — because a transition that builds the panels without the batteries and power lines would produce a grid that is cleaner on paper and shakier in practice.

The evidence that matters most here is the National Renewable Energy Laboratory's finding, because it is the only source that directly answers the feasibility question rather than describing the present. NREL concludes there are "several ways" to reach a net-zero grid by 2035 at reasonable cost — but its conclusion comes with a condition: the grid works only if the country spends, at scale, on storage and thousands of miles of new transmission. That condition is the whole argument. The lab explicitly reframes the question as "economic and political... not purely scientific." In other words, the source that carries the most weight in this debate tells us the debate is no longer about physics.
